    35→- [x] `ramseyR3` is marked `noncomputable` (uses `sInf`, which involves classical choice).
    36→- [x] No `sorry` in the definition.

    38→- N/A: No `local notation` used (none needed).

    47→- [x] Both theorems have docstrings describing the problem and citing source papers.

    53→
    54→- [x] Theorem type is `P` directly (no `answer()` wrapper): `Tendsto (fun k => ...) atTop atTop`.

    61→- [x] `answer(True)` would mean "yes, it is o(k)" and `answer(False)` would mean "no".

The two-part problem is correctly split into a Case 3 direct assertion (Part 1: "Show that ...") and a Case 1 yes/no question (Part 2: "prove or disprove"), with appropriate `answer()` usage for each. The custom definition `ramseyR3` is properly documented and marked `noncomputable`. Bibliography references `[Er81c]` and `[Er93]` are cited in both the module docstring and theorem docstrings.

# Formal Conjectures PR Checklist — `deepmind/544.lean`

Every item must pass before a PR is approved. Reviewers: reject if any box is unchecked
without an explicit justification.

---

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line): `import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line.
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erdős Problem 544`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to `erdosproblems.com/544`.
- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem (R(3,k+1) − R(3,k) → ∞ and the little-o question).
- [x] Bibliography entries present: `[Er81c]` and `[Er93]`.

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos544 ... end Erdos544`.
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase` (`Erdos544`).
- [x] `open` statements appear **before** the namespace block: `open SimpleGraph Filter`.

## 5. Custom Definitions

- [x] `ramseyR3` (line 41) has a LaTeX docstring (lines 38–40) describing R(3,k).
- [x] `ramseyR3` is marked `noncomputable` (uses `sInf`, which involves classical choice).
- [x] No `sorry` in the definition.
- [x] Function returning a value uses `lowerCamelCase`: `ramseyR3`.
- N/A: No `local notation` used (none needed).
- N/A: No `@[category test]`/`@[category API]` sanity checks (optional; "consider" item).

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Both statements use the `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_544` (snake_case, matches namespace number).
- [x] Variant named `erdos_544.variants.little_o` (follows `erdos_N.variants.<descriptor>` convention).
- [x] Each theorem has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research open, AMS 5]`.
- [x] Both theorems have docstrings describing the problem and citing source papers.
- [x] Docstrings reference source papers: `[Er81c]` and `[Er93, p. 339]`.

### `answer()` usage

**Part 1** (`erdos_544`, line 53): The problem says "Show that R(3,k+1) − R(3,k) → ∞" — a direct assertion ("Show that ..."), so **Case 3** applies.

- [x] Theorem type is `P` directly (no `answer()` wrapper): `Tendsto (fun k => ...) atTop atTop`.
- [x] No quantifiers as theorem parameters; the statement is self-contained.

**Part 2** (`erdos_544.variants.little_o`, line 65): The problem says "prove or disprove that R(3,k+1) − R(3,k) = o(k)" — a yes/no question ("prove or disprove"), so **Case 1** applies.

- [x] Theorem type is `answer(sorry) ↔ P`.
- [x] The RHS (`P`) formalizes the question as originally asked: `Tendsto (fun k => ((ramseyR3 (k+1) : ℝ) - (ramseyR3 k : ℝ)) / (k : ℝ)) atTop (nhds 0)`, correctly capturing (R(3,k+1) − R(3,k))/k → 0.
- [x] `answer(True)` would mean "yes, it is o(k)" and `answer(False)` would mean "no".
- [x] Quantifiers go **after** `answer(sorry)` (inside the RHS of `↔`), not as theorem parameters.

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has **exactly one** `@[category ...]` attribute: `category research open`.
- [x] `research open` is a valid category value.
- [x] Both theorems contain `sorry` in their proof bodies (consistent with `research open`).

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has **exactly one** `@[AMS ...]` attribute: `AMS 5`.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed (5).
- N/A: Ordering and duplicates — only one number, so trivially satisfied.
- [x] 5 (combinatorics) is a valid AMS MSC2020 code.

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Both use the multi-line `by\n  sorry` format (acceptable per checklist).
- [x] No `sorry` in `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations.
- [x] No informal proof sketches for these open research problems.
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- [x] Variant named `erdos_544.variants.little_o` (follows naming convention).
- [x] Variant lives in the **same file** as the main theorem.
- [x] Variant has its own `@[category research open, AMS 5]` attributes.
- [x] Variant has a docstring describing the little-o question.

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used where applicable: `ℕ`, `ℤ`, `ℝ`, `∀`, `¬`, `↔`.
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` that disables linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all under 100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] `lake build` not run during this review. No obvious issues; expected `sorry` warnings only.
- [ ] Custom linters not run during this review.

---

## Summary

**Result: PASS** (all substantive items pass)

The file is clean, well-structured, and fully conformant. The two-part problem is correctly split into a Case 3 direct assertion (Part 1: "Show that ...") and a Case 1 yes/no question (Part 2: "prove or disprove"), with appropriate `answer()` usage for each. The custom definition `ramseyR3` is properly documented and marked `noncomputable`. Bibliography references `[Er81c]` and `[Er93]` are cited in both the module docstring and theorem docstrings.

One note:
1. **Build verification** (§12): Not performed during this review; should be confirmed in CI.

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 544
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/544](https://www.erdosproblems.com/544)
    23→
    24→Show that $R(3,k+1) - R(3,k) \to \infty$ as $k \to \infty$.
    25→Similarly, prove or disprove that $R(3,k+1) - R(3,k) = o(k)$.
    26→
    27→A problem of Erdős and Sós. See also [165] and [1014].
    28→
    29→[Er81c] Erdős, P., _Some problems in combinatorics, graph theory and probability_ (1981).
    30→
    31→[Er93] Erdős, P., _Some of my favourite problems in various branches of combinatorics_ (1993), p. 339.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open SimpleGraph Filter
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos544
    37→
    38→/-- The Ramsey number $R(3,k)$: the minimum $N$ such that every simple graph
    39→on $N$ vertices contains either a triangle ($3$-clique) or an independent
    40→set of size $k$ (a $k$-clique in the complement). -/
    41→noncomputable def ramseyR3 (k : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    42→  sInf {N : ℕ | ∀ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin N)), ¬G.CliqueFree 3 ∨ ¬Gᶜ.CliqueFree k}
    43→
    44→/--
    45→Erdős Problem 544 (Part 1) [Er81c][Er93, p. 339]:
    46→
    47→Show that $R(3,k+1) - R(3,k) \to \infty$ as $k \to \infty$.
    48→
    49→That is, the consecutive differences of the Ramsey numbers $R(3,k)$
    50→tend to infinity.
    51→-/
    52→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    53→theorem erdos_544 :
    54→    Tendsto (fun k : ℕ => (ramseyR3 (k + 1) : ℤ) - (ramseyR3 k : ℤ))
    55→      atTop atTop := by
    56→  sorry
    57→
    58→/--
    59→Erdős Problem 544 (Part 2) [Er81c][Er93, p. 339]:
    60→
    61→Prove or disprove that $R(3,k+1) - R(3,k) = o(k)$, i.e.,
    62→$(R(3,k+1) - R(3,k)) / k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$.
    63→-/
    64→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    65→theorem erdos_544.variants.little_o : answer(sorry) ↔
    66→    Tendsto (fun k : ℕ => ((ramseyR3 (k + 1) : ℝ) - (ramseyR3 k : ℝ)) / (k : ℝ))
    67→      atTop (nhds 0) := by
    68→  sorry
    69→
    70→end Erdos544
    71→

file.content:
/-
Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
You may obtain a copy of the License at

    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
limitations under the License.
-/

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erdős Problem 544

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/544](https://www.erdosproblems.com/544)

Show that $R(3,k+1) - R(3,k) \to \infty$ as $k \to \infty$.
Similarly, prove or disprove that $R(3,k+1) - R(3,k) = o(k)$.

A problem of Erdős and Sós. See also [165] and [1014].

[Er81c] Erdős, P., _Some problems in combinatorics, graph theory and probability_ (1981).

[Er93] Erdős, P., _Some of my favourite problems in various branches of combinatorics_ (1993), p. 339.
-/

open SimpleGraph Filter

namespace Erdos544

/-- The Ramsey number $R(3,k)$: the minimum $N$ such that every simple graph
on $N$ vertices contains either a triangle ($3$-clique) or an independent
set of size $k$ (a $k$-clique in the complement). -/
noncomputable def ramseyR3 (k : ℕ) : ℕ :=
  sInf {N : ℕ | ∀ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin N)), ¬G.CliqueFree 3 ∨ ¬Gᶜ.CliqueFree k}

/--
Erdős Problem 544 (Part 1) [Er81c][Er93, p. 339]:

Show that $R(3,k+1) - R(3,k) \to \infty$ as $k \to \infty$.

That is, the consecutive differences of the Ramsey numbers $R(3,k)$
tend to infinity.
-/
@[category research open, AMS 5]
theorem erdos_544 :
    Tendsto (fun k : ℕ => (ramseyR3 (k + 1) : ℤ) - (ramseyR3 k : ℤ))
      atTop atTop := by
  sorry

/--
Erdős Problem 544 (Part 2) [Er81c][Er93, p. 339]:

Prove or disprove that $R(3,k+1) - R(3,k) = o(k)$, i.e.,
$(R(3,k+1) - R(3,k)) / k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$.
-/
@[category research open, AMS 5]
theorem erdos_544.variants.little_o : answer(sorry) ↔
    Tendsto (fun k : ℕ => ((ramseyR3 (k + 1) : ℝ) - (ramseyR3 k : ℝ)) / (k : ℝ))
      atTop (nhds 0) := by
  sorry

end Erdos544

content.text: The review passed — **Result: PASS**. No changes needed.
